findings.vulnerability: PII scrubber does not scrub request body
findings.recommendation: scrubRequest must recursively walk body and apply redactPii to all string fields

findings.vulnerability: OpenAI key regex {20,} allows 19-char keys to leak
findings.recommendation: verify against actual OpenAI key format: sk-[A-Za-z0-9]{20,}T3BlbkFJ... (real keys are 51 chars)

findings.vulnerability: IPv4 addresses not scrubbed (may be PII per GDPR)
findings.evidence: IP passes through: server 192.168.1.100 logged the request

findings.vulnerability: Gap registry has no signature — forgeries trivial
findings.evidence: wrote forged entry directly to .ndjson, accepted with no validation
findings.recommendation: sign entries with HMAC like RedTeamBaseline does; reject tampered files on load

findings.vulnerability: RecoveryVerifier calls bootstrap() once per layer (4× in full battery) without checking if fault actually injected
findings.evidence: 4 layers → 4 bootstrap calls. If bootstrap is heavy (e.g., DB migration), this is a self-DoS
findings.recommendation: gate bootstrap on actual fault detection; pass `faultInjected` flag from runLayer to verifyAndRecover

findings.vulnerability: Attacker prompt echoes baseline payload verbatim — no size cap
findings.recommendation: cap baseline payload to 4KB before injecting into prompt

findings.vulnerability: Plugin supply chain scenarios contain prompt-injection payloads in their description field — they render in any UI that displays scenario name/description
findings.evidence: 2 scenarios with injection-shaped payloads in description field
findings.recommendation: use unique synthetic IDs (e.g. PLUGIN-SUPPLY-002.example) instead of injection-style payloads in the description
